The Long‑Term Cognitive Consequences of Heavy AI Reliance and Their Implications for Education
Generative AI (GenAI) can dramatically accelerate routine tasks, but prolonged, uncritical use erodes the
very neural circuitry that underpins memory, problem‑solving, creativity, and executive function. Educational
institutions must therefore move from “AI‑enabled” to
learning environments that preserve and nurture deep
83 % of ChatGPT users could not recall a sentence they had just written; working‑memory retention fell
Knowledge workers who trust GenAI report lower perceived mental effort and lower critical‑thinking
AI‑generated essays were faster (≈60 % speedup) but described as “robotic” and “soulless”; top
performers were those who used AI
Neural activity in the LLM group dropped 47 % relative to the brain‑only group; ownership of the text
Habitual cognitive offloading leads to “lazy thinkers” (Georgiou 2025 experiment) and lower
Sources: MIT Media Lab, UT Austin/Georgia Tech/Hugging Face, Carnegie Mellon/Cambridge, Frontiers 2024, Psychology Today
These findings converge on a single concept:
—the gradual weakening of neural pathways when critical mental
